title: Cat Sneezing and Green Nasal Discharge: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat sneezing with green discharge? Learn what it means, the urgency level for vet care, and why photos help with veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green or yellow nasal discharge in cats usually indicates a bacterial infection that requires a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent progression to pneumonia.

What does it mean if my cat has green or yellow nasal discharge?

When a cat has green or yellow nasal discharge, it is often a sign of a secondary bacterial infection. While clear discharge can be related to simple allergies or a mild viral flare-up (like Feline Herpesvirus), thick and colored mucus indicates that the immune system is actively fighting an infection in the upper respiratory tract or sinuses.

Is green nasal discharge in my cat a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for this condition is Medium; it is generally not a life-threatening emergency unless your cat is having significant difficulty breathing or has stopped eating entirely.
  • Bacterial infections in the respiratory tract rarely resolve on their own.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the condition from progressing to pneumonia.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's nasal discharge for the veterinarian?

  • Providing a clear, close-up photo is extremely beneficial because cats often groom themselves and wipe away mucus before they arrive at the clinic.
  • A photo ensures the vet sees the true color, consistency, and volume of the discharge.
  • This visual evidence helps the vet determine the severity of the infection and whether immediate diagnostic imaging or specific antibiotics are required.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Acute rhinitis in cats is characterized by nasal discharge, sneezing, pawing at the face, respiratory stertor, open-mouth breathing, and/or inspiratory dyspnea. Lacrimation and conjunctivitis may accompany upper respiratory inflammation. The nasal discharge is initially serous, becoming mucoid due to secondary bacterial infection or mucopurulent if inflammatory cells infiltrate the mucosa. Chronic nasal discharge that was initially unilateral but becomes bilateral, or changes in character from mucopurulent to serosanguineous or hemorrhagic, suggests neoplastic or mycotic disease. Approximately 35% of cats with nasal cryptococcosis have facial deformity of the rostral aspect of the nose. Diagnosis is based on history, physical examination, radiographic findings (especially CT), rhinoscopy, nasal biopsy, deep nasal tissue culture, and elimination of other causes of nasal discharge and sneezing.

Chapter: Respiratory, Infectious Diseases, Neoplasia, Fungal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1489)

faqs.question: Can I use human cold medicine for my cat?
faqs.answer: No, never give your cat human medications. Many over-the-counter decongestants and pain relievers are highly toxic to felines.

faqs.question: What are common causes of colored discharge?
faqs.answer: Common causes include feline respiratory disease complex (cat flu), nasal polyps, foreign bodies stuck in the nose, or advanced dental disease.

faqs.question: Is green nasal discharge contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, many upper respiratory infections are highly contagious between cats. It is best to isolate the symptomatic cat until they have been evaluated by a vet.
